mnemonics.title: Remember alarm() basics
mnemonics.content: ALARM = A Like A Real-time Message - It Arrives after econds to iMplement timeouts. Key: alarm() uses seconds, delivers SIGALRM, returns remaining seconds.

mnemonics.title: Pause function
mnemonics.content: PAUSE = Pause hALTs Until nSignal Arrives. pause() waits for ANY signal to interrupt it.

mnemonics.title: setitimer timer types
mnemonics.content: RVP = Real = SIGALRM, Virtual = SIGVTALRM, Profile = SIGPROF. Think 'RVP - Really Valuable for Profiling'.

acronyms.usage: Delivered by alarm() and ITIMER_REAL when timer expires
Delivered by ITIMER_VIRTUAL when user-mode time expires
Delivered by ITIMER_PROF for profiling CPU usage
Timer mechanism for generating periodic or one-shot signals

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: at time 0, call alarm(5); at time 5, SIGALRM fires; if alarm(3) called at time 2, old alarm cancelled and new one fires at time 5
Show three columns: ITIMER_REAL (real clock), ITIMER_VIRTUAL (user CPU), ITIMER_PROF (user+system). Each has it_value (current) and it_interval (reload) fields.
Draw flow: 1) Check if data available, 2) Signal arrives here (race window), 3) Call blocking read - if signal came first, we block forever!

analogies.analogy: Like setting a kitchen timer - you set it for X minutes and when it rings (SIGALRM), you know that time has passed.
Like waiting by your phone for ANY call to wake you up - you won't know who called until you answer (handle the signal).
alarm() is like a simple egg timer (seconds only), while setitimer() is like a sophisticated stopwatch with lap times and different modes.
